jokes.text: Not his bio father, his daddi with an 'I'.
jokes.setup: Abbi asks if Jaime is visiting his father in Guatemala, implying a biological parent
jokes.punchline: Ilana clarifies it's a 'daddi with an I' — a sugar daddy — delivered completely matter-of-factly
jokes.explanation: The wordplay is sharp and the deadpan delivery is quintessential Ilana — she treats Jaime's unconventional arrangement as totally unremarkable. 'Daddi with an I' is a genuinely clever construction that earns its laugh through character specificity rather than shock alone.

jokes.text: Ilana, I'm not doing another séance. It's like, weird and kinda Christian.
jokes.setup: Abbi hears Ilana has the place to herself and immediately assumes the worst
jokes.punchline: The specific grievance — not that séances are spooky or annoying, but that they're 'kinda Christian' — is an unexpected, very Abbi-coded complaint
jokes.explanation: The joke works on two levels: the implied history of prior séances, and the specific objection being theological rather than practical. 'Kinda Christian' is funny because it's so specific and so Abbi — vaguely secular Jewish anxiety expressed sideways. Strong character integration.

jokes.text: I'm gonna sublet my apartment on BnBNYC, so I can make some money 'cause I don't have a jerb right now.
jokes.setup: Ilana is explaining her practical financial plan
jokes.punchline: Drops 'jerb' — a comedically lazy pronunciation of 'job' — as if it's standard vocabulary, undercutting any pretense of adult responsibility
jokes.explanation: 'Jerb' is a recurring Broad City verbal tic — functional and character-consistent but not especially crafted. It gets a smile from familiarity more than construction.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! I forgot that Melody was my actual roommate.
jokes.setup: Abbi says Bevers is away, implying she has the apartment to herself
jokes.punchline: She has entirely forgotten her actual roommate Melody exists — a running gag about Bevers being so omnipresent he overshadows the real tenant
jokes.explanation: This joke does double work: it's funny on its face (Abbi literally forgot her roommate exists) and it's a payoff for the long-running Bevers joke — his presence is so consuming it has psychologically displaced the actual tenant from Abbi's consciousness. Rewatch bonus for how cleanly it's structured.

jokes.text: I need to re-sole my loafers. / I have a cavus foot. / High arches. / Ballerinas have it.
jokes.setup: Ilana says Abbi could make more money subletting her apartment — pivotal financial decision
jokes.punchline: Abbi's entire justification for needing money is a shoe sole repair caused by a foot condition she then explains in loving, unnecessary detail, with both of them chiming in about ballerinas
jokes.explanation: The comedy is in the disproportion — the grandiosity of the financial scheme contrasted with its deeply mundane motivation (shoe repair for a medical-grade foot condition). The synced 'ballerinas have it' callback is a nice small moment of the two operating on the same wavelength.

jokes.text: 'Queen artisan's nook in tolerable Gowanus. Cockroach-free, except for in the bathroom and the kitchen,' in smaller font.
jokes.setup: Ilana is writing her BnBNYC listing, aiming for aspirational marketing copy
jokes.punchline: 'Tolerable Gowanus' is already damning, then the cockroach disclosure is put in smaller font — legally disclosing while marketing against it
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely excellent joke construction. 'Tolerable Gowanus' alone is great — a brutal admission dressed as a sell. Then 'cockroach-free, except for in the bathroom and the kitchen' is a perfect escalation: the exception eliminates the claim entirely. Putting it 'in smaller font' adds a layer of bureaucratic bad faith that makes it a triple-decker joke. Economical and very character-specific to Ilana's deluded self-confidence.

jokes.text: 'Shabby chic meets Brooklyn funk.' Um, I have to say that 'cause of the smell.
jokes.setup: Ilana continues her listing with aspirational design language
jokes.punchline: Immediately confesses the entire 'Brooklyn funk' framing is a legal/PR cover for an actual odor problem
jokes.explanation: Follows nicely from the cockroach joke — Ilana's apartment keeps revealing new problems. The 'cause of the smell' aside has perfect comic economy; the hedging 'Um' before the confession makes it feel improvised and real.

jokes.text: 'Treat yourself to a night in picturesque Astoria. Stop by the beer garden, or stay in and enjoy tasteful interiors and a stunning view of a white... castle.'
jokes.setup: Abbi writes an aspirational listing that sounds genuinely nice — beer garden, tasteful interiors, stunning view
jokes.punchline: The pause before 'castle' and then the reveal it's a White Castle fast food restaurant directly outside
jokes.explanation: Strong structural joke — the deliberate pause on 'white... castle' is doing real work, letting the audience run briefly with a grander image before the deflation. The misdirection is clean and economical. Abbi's earnest delivery makes the self-deception funnier.

jokes.text: I'm gonna post this for $700 a night. / Dude, that is almost a month's rent. / No one's gonna do that. / It's New York, babe. Get used to it, babe. Hashtag, tenement life!
jokes.setup: Abbi's reasonable objection to the absurd price point
jokes.punchline: Ilana's escalating NYC-hustle affirmations — 'Get used to it, babe' and 'Hashtag, tenement life!' — delivered as though she's a savvy entrepreneur rather than someone subletting a cockroach-infested smell-apartment
jokes.explanation: 'Hashtag, tenement life' is the kicker — the word 'tenement' doing all the work, recasting dire poverty conditions as aspirational branding. Ilana's oblivious confidence is well-deployed here.

jokes.text: Oh, my lady God, thank you.
jokes.setup: Ilana is seeing her rental requests flood in
jokes.punchline: 'My lady God' — Ilana's feminist theological rebranding of a standard exclamation
jokes.explanation: Very Ilana — the ideological retooling of casual divine invocations. Small joke, lands quietly, but it's a nice texture moment.

jokes.text: Oh, of course you love camping, 'cause you probably went camping with your father down the Main Line River and roasted crackers over a fire you built.
jokes.setup: Abbi says she loves camping
jokes.punchline: Ilana's mocking class-based projection — the specific detail of 'crackers over a fire you built' paints Abbi as an outdoorsy WASP with a father who taught her survivalism
jokes.explanation: The class ribbing is very Broad City — Ilana clocking Abbi's privileged upbringing. 'Crackers' specifically is a funny choice over marshmallows or s'mores; the slightly wrong camping snack makes the whole image sharper.

jokes.text: Ilana doing a full stoner-festival-bum impression: 'Oh, can I bum some gas money, man? I'm just getting right up at hacky sack, man, I'm good, man. Jeepers creepers, man. I need that gas money, m...'
jokes.setup: Ilana is mocking Abbi's supposed Phish/Bonnaroo camping background
jokes.punchline: She launches into a sustained festival-bum character voice, escalating through 'Jeepers creepers' — an incongruously wholesome exclamation — and trailing off mid-word
jokes.explanation: 'Jeepers creepers, man' is the funniest specific detail — the squeaky-clean expression delivered in a burnout voice. The trailing-off is a physical comedy moment that works in performance.

jokes.text: WiFi network is Halal House, password is 'PitaGuest' one word. But the signal is barely there, so you gotta get right up on the window.
jokes.setup: Ilana is giving her French subletter a welcome orientation
jokes.punchline: The WiFi is stolen from the halal restaurant next door, and it barely reaches her apartment — presented as an amenity
jokes.explanation: 'PitaGuest' is a perfect detail — specific enough to feel completely real. Presenting stolen, barely-functional WiFi as a listed amenity is great Ilana logic. The specific instruction to press against the window is a visual comedy bonus.

jokes.text: And as my ad stated, this rental is all-inclusive, so please enjoy some dank ganj. My treat. / Mmm... Colorado.
jokes.setup: Ilana presents the 'all-inclusive' amenity of her apartment
jokes.punchline: The amenity is weed, which she hands to the subletter, who immediately identifies the strain's state of origin
jokes.explanation: The guest's single reverential 'Mmm... Colorado' is a perfect button — the sommelier energy applied to weed, simultaneously characterizing both Ilana (she buys quality product to share with strangers) and her European guest (who evaluates it like a wine).

jokes.text: Ilana cutting to herself looking mortified/apologetic — 'I'm really sorry' — to a different guest, implying the apartment horrified them.
jokes.setup: After showing an appreciative French guest around, we see a beat with Ilana clearly facing someone else's horror at the apartment.
jokes.punchline: The unspoken 'I'm really sorry' implies we're seeing multiple guests react very differently — the comedy is in what we DON'T see.
jokes.explanation: A quick visual/reaction beat that relies on implication. Economy is high — two words do all the work. The montage structure suggests her apartment quality varies wildly depending on the guest's standards. Effective but minor.

jokes.text: 'This is the bedroom. You probably would call it a boudoir.' / 'Très sympa, très très très chouette.'
jokes.setup: Ilana presents her cramped apartment bedroom
jokes.punchline: She elevates it with the French word 'boudoir'; the French guy responds with escalating superlatives ('very very very nice') that feel both earnest and faintly absurd given what we can infer the room looks like
jokes.explanation: The irony of calling a Gowanus bedroom a 'boudoir' in front of a French person — who then responds politely — is a quiet joke. Functional.

jokes.text: This is a map I drew of Astoria, the 'hood. The knife and fork mean it's an affordable restaurant, and this is a machete. This is where someone machete'd another person in half, so I would just avoid that spot.
jokes.setup: Abbi is orienting her subletter with a hand-drawn neighborhood map
jokes.punchline: The map contains a hand-drawn machete symbol marking a specific local violent crime, delivered in the same casual tone as the restaurant guide
jokes.explanation: The tonal flatness is perfect — the machete warning sits in the exact same register as restaurant recommendations, treating a violent crime as equivalent civic information for the tourist. 'Machete'd another person in half' is horrific and funny in equal measure. Strong structure: the map legend setup, then the legend item that shouldn't exist.

jokes.text: Oh, okay, so this is a disposable camera. I took half the photos already and I was thinking maybe you could take the other half, and then I would get them developed and then, I could send them to you.
jokes.setup: Abbi presents another 'amenity' to her subletter
jokes.punchline: She has already used half a disposable camera she's now giving to a stranger to complete, as if this is a lovely shared experience rather than a mildly insane proposition
jokes.explanation: This is beautifully absurd and character-specific to Abbi's earnest, slightly artsy, completely unself-aware hospitality. The logistics she describes — getting them developed, mailing them — are painstakingly mundane for a transaction with a stranger who will never see her again.

jokes.text: Perhaps you will come in Paris one day and we'll do it both ways. / I love both ways. Like, any way, I would do it, whatever way. I mean, with the camera. With the camera. A thing that I established.
jokes.setup: The French subletter responds to the disposable camera idea romantically — 'perhaps you'll come to Paris and we'll do it both ways'
jokes.punchline: Abbi's increasingly panicked clarifications — 'with the camera, with the camera, A THING THAT I ESTABLISHED' — confirm she heard the sexual double entendre even if he didn't mean it
jokes.explanation: 'A thing that I established' is the comedic peak — Abbi appealing to the formal record of her own camera proposal as though that resolves the ambiguity. The escalating self-correction is great cringe structure.

jokes.text: The French subletter picks lint off Abbi's top while she freezes, then: 'How you say? Do you say leent? / It's lint.'
jokes.setup: The already-charged interaction is interrupted by the subletter stepping close to Abbi
jokes.punchline: He is merely removing lint and asking about the English word — but the intimate gesture and the earnest pronunciation question ('leent?') in the wake of 'both ways' makes the mundane act charged and funny
jokes.explanation: The scene plants tension from the 'both ways' joke and pays it off with a completely innocent act, but the visual intimacy and Abbi's frozen reaction keep the comedy alive. 'Leent?' is a perfectly observed ESL moment that somehow makes it sweeter and funnier.

jokes.text: It's like we're really camping. Even the wind feels real, right? / The wind's real. / It just feels like we're not in 'the shitty.'
jokes.setup: They're on Abbi's urban rooftop, trying to frame it as a romantic camping experience
jokes.punchline: Abbi's reassurance that the wind 'feels real' — because it IS real — and Ilana's 'the shitty' (the city) as a term for urban misery, revealing the depths of their self-delusion
jokes.explanation: The 'the wind's real' deadpan response to Abbi's 'it feels real' is nicely underplayed. 'The shitty' is a cute piece of Ilana vocabulary. Functional rooftop-poverty comedy.

jokes.text: The first time I ever saw any sex stuff was at my friend Amina Eltreebee's birthday party. / Did she... do it right in front of you? / No. We watched 'Fear.' Mark Wahlberg, Reese Witherspoon on a roller coaster, he's like... / He's fingering her. / Fingering her.
jokes.setup: Rooftop sleepover intimacy leads to childhood sleepover memory sharing
jokes.punchline: The story builds as a sex ed anecdote — then pivots to 'Fear' with the specific roller coaster fingering scene, with Abbi cutting in to complete the sentence as though this is a shared cultural touchstone
jokes.explanation: The synced punchline — Abbi completing 'he's fingering her' — makes the joke. It suggests this scene from 'Fear' was a universal formative moment for their generation, and Abbi's immediate recognition is both funny and oddly touching. Very character-specific to how these two operate.

jokes.text: And then we all thought we had to pee, but I think everyone was just really horny.
jokes.setup: Ilana finishes the 'Fear' story
jokes.punchline: The childhood sexual awakening described through the lens of confusion about bodily functions — presented casually as a universal discovery
jokes.explanation: This is genuinely great. Economical, specific, character-perfect for Ilana (who talks about sex as naturally as weather), and it gets at something true about prepubescent confusion in a way that's both funny and uncomfortably relatable. 'I think everyone was just really horny' as a corrective to 'we thought we had to pee' — the casual recalibration — is the joke. High craft.

jokes.text: I guess I... / Still confuse that now? / Yeah!
jokes.setup: Ilana's line about childhood confusion between arousal and needing to pee
jokes.punchline: Abbi's unfinished confession, Ilana's guess, and Abbi's delighted confirmation — the joke escalates by revealing this isn't just a childhood confusion for at least one of them
jokes.explanation: Perfect two-beat callback structure: Ilana's story ends, Abbi starts a sentence she can't finish, Ilana guesses 'still confuse that now?' and Abbi's enthusiastic 'Yeah!' is the payoff. The economy is exceptional — four words complete a joke that started two lines ago. Strong callback that deepens the original joke.

jokes.text: Wake up in the middle of the night, her stepdad is watching us sleep in his undies. / Stop it. / Eating lasagna.
jokes.setup: Abbi begins a sleepover memory that follows Ilana's — the parallel structure sets up a 'top that' dynamic
jokes.punchline: 'Eating lasagna.' The stepdad watching sleeping children in his underwear is already unsettling; the lasagna transforms it into something grotesque and absurd — the specific wrongness of that particular food at that particular moment
jokes.explanation: This is the best constructed joke in the episode. The delivery in three beats — setup, beat, 'Eating lasagna' — is masterful. 'Eating lasagna' is two words that do extraordinary comedic work: they add specificity (why lasagna?), they make an already creepy image suddenly both more mundane and more wrong, and the word 'lasagna' has an inherently funny sound. Ilana's 'Stop it' before the kicker shows the audience surrogate reaction. The punchline escalation from creepy to absurdly creepy to somehow-funnier is exceptional. Comparable to the anchor 8.0 examples — arguably exceeds them in economy.

jokes.text: 'I just pretended to be asleep until he was finished.' / 'What do you mean, until he was finished?' / 'Oh, finished the lasagna. But still, it was creepy, you know?'
jokes.setup: 'Until he was finished' — Ilana (and the audience) reads the worst possible interpretation
jokes.punchline: He finished the lasagna. But Abbi's 'but still, it was creepy' preserves the horror of the original image while deflating the worst-case read
jokes.explanation: This is a callback AND a misdirection AND a second punchline. 'Finished' is a genuine double-meaning trap: Ilana's alarmed 'what do you mean until he was finished?' voices the audience's fear. The clarification is both a relief and its own horror (he ate lasagna for HOW long watching them sleep?). 'But still, it was creepy' is a beautiful deadpan understatement. The sequence of jokes 24-25 is the episode's comedic peak.

jokes.text: The suburbs, there's like some 'American Beauty' shit going on every corner.
jokes.setup: The conclusion of the creepy stepdad story
jokes.punchline: Ilana contextualizes it as a general suburban condition, citing 'American Beauty' — which, given the film's themes, is an absurdly apt and funny reference
jokes.explanation: The 'American Beauty' reference is apt but not novel — it's the kind of cultural shorthand that does its job without adding much. Button on the stronger jokes that precede it.

jokes.text: The tent collapses on them on the roof.
jokes.setup: They've been successfully camping on the roof and the mood has been pleasant
jokes.punchline: The tent collapses, puncturing the illusion of their outdoor adventure
jokes.explanation: Visual slapstick — works in performance even if it reads simply on the page. The 'Hold down the base!' urgency is a funny touch. Not a high-craft joke but earns a decent reaction from the physical comedy.

jokes.text: Dude, that guy on the gurney is shaking. / On the flipside, he's on a gurney. He's not in a body bag. / That's so true.
jokes.setup: The tent collapse apparently alerted someone, leading to emergency services being called; the aftermath includes a person on a gurney
jokes.punchline: Ilana's consolation — 'at least he's not in a body bag' — as the silver lining, with Abbi finding this genuinely reassuring
jokes.explanation: The low bar of 'not in a body bag' as a comfort is dark and funny. 'That's so true' from Abbi playing along earnestly makes it work — both of them choosing the most generous possible reading of a bad situation.

jokes.text: In that case, I should definitely come over 'cause I am a greasy third wheel. Pop a finger in there.
jokes.setup: Lincoln tells Ilana he has a woman over, presumably to discourage the request
jokes.punchline: Ilana reverses the logic — his having a date makes her MORE eager to come — and 'pop a finger in there' is an extraordinary overshare/proposition
jokes.explanation: 'Pop a finger in there' is pure Ilana — the phrasing is so casually specific, and her immediate pivot from 'I'm the third wheel' to sexual proposition is delivered without self-consciousness, which is exactly the character. The inversion of Lincoln's news (meant to deter her) into an invitation is also a good structural subversion. High character integration.

jokes.text: No, don't... Don't be sorry. This is healthy.
jokes.setup: Lincoln apologizes for not being available
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'This is healthy' — reassuring herself and him while clearly feeling something — the affirmation is too emphatic to be convincing
jokes.explanation: This is a small but excellent character joke — Ilana's coping mechanism is to narrate her feelings in therapy-speak while actively suppressing them. 'This is healthy' as a non-sequitur farewell is perfect.

jokes.text: I'll be right out. From slowly peeing. Definitely not pooping.
jokes.setup: Lincoln hangs up with Ilana, then addresses someone off-phone
jokes.punchline: He is clearly pooping and announces the slower-than-expected exit with an overly specific denial — 'definitely not pooping'
jokes.explanation: 'Definitely not pooping' is both too specific (a denial so targeted it confirms the thing) and oddly endearing — Lincoln is managing multiple social awkwardnesses at once. The structure of establishing 'slowly peeing' as the cover story before undercutting it is clean.

jokes.text: Eliot is in Jersey, 'cause Attica's actually got a huge Purina audition.
jokes.setup: They're working through their contact list to find somewhere to stay
jokes.punchline: Eliot isn't in Jersey by choice — he's there for his dog's commercial audition, presented as a valid serious commitment
jokes.explanation: The specificity of 'Purina audition' — a real dog food brand, a real career opportunity in Ilana's universe — and going to New Jersey for it is a nice texture joke. Not a big laugh but earns a smile.

jokes.text: Turn on the old noggin. Come on, give me something good, girl. / Here we go! Think, think, think, think, think, think, think...
jokes.setup: They need to figure out where to stay all night
jokes.punchline: The 'thinking' is performed as a full physical act with audible narration — 'think think think think' — deployed with complete earnestness
jokes.explanation: The visual of Ilana physically performing the act of thinking is a recurring broad comedy beat — works in performance, mid-range craft.

jokes.text: What if we go to that bar where we got mono?
jokes.setup: They're brainstorming places to kill time all night in NYC
jokes.punchline: The bar is not identified by name, neighborhood, or any normal quality — it's identified by the disease they contracted there, and Ilana loves it
jokes.explanation: Economical and character-perfect. The bar's entire identity is the illness you got there, and Ilana says 'I love that place!' without irony. The intimacy of shared-illness-as-fond-memory is a very specific Broad City friendship note.

jokes.text: Watch the sunrise and get a romantic breakfast at the diner. / Not the bad diner, though. / Hate the bad diner. / It is close to everything, though. Should we just go to that one?
jokes.setup: They're planning a romantic all-nighter
jokes.punchline: The immediate capitulation to the bad diner — hated, acknowledged as bad, but 'close to everything' — so they'll go anyway
jokes.explanation: The rhythm here is great — the rapid 'Hate the bad diner' / 'should we just go to that one?' capitulation is very true to how people make decisions, and true to these characters specifically.

jokes.text: ♪ I'm nuts for nuts! ♪
jokes.setup: Scene transition to a street nuts vendor
jokes.punchline: Ilana sings a jingle celebrating roasted nuts with genuine enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Small texture joke — Ilana's unselfconscious food enthusiasm expressed through spontaneous jingle. Works because of performance energy.

jokes.text: These taste pretty good, but they smell amazing. Like, I would wear it as a scent.
jokes.setup: Abbi is eating roasted nuts from a cart
jokes.punchline: The escalation from 'good taste' to 'great smell' to 'I would wear it as a perfume' — the leap from snack to fragrance is funny in its conviction
jokes.explanation: Mild observational humor — the NYC street nut phenomenon is real and the scent-perfume idea is the kind of absurd escalation these two do naturally.

jokes.text: Dude, my hot French subletter is texting me asking my birthday. Maybe he wants to send me flowers, or, like, find out my zodiac sign to see if we're compatible. Euros are so into numerology. Madonna got really into it when she moved to London.
jokes.setup: Abbi is interpreting a text from her subletter asking for her birthday
jokes.punchline: The chain of increasingly reaching explanations — flowers, zodiac compatibility, numerology, Madonna in London — when the actual reason will turn out to be sinister (he needs her birthday for her PIN/combination)
jokes.explanation: The joke works on first watch as a character moment (Abbi's romantic projection), but gains rewatch value when we know the subletter was robbing her. 'Madonna got really into it when she moved to London' is a randomly specific celebrity reference used as evidence that lands well. The escalating logic chain is Abbi at her most endearingly self-deluded.

jokes.text: Christina! Yes, you picked up! Uh, yeah, it's at the 40/40 Club, all right? Yeah, go up to the bouncer, say you're there for the Hernandez party, okay? Free bottles. Hernandez! / I'm gonna get you back, girl! / Opportunity, batch! Free bottles? We gotta take advantage of this.
jokes.setup: They overhear a stranger on the phone giving out free access to a club event
jokes.punchline: They immediately decide to use this information to crash the Hernandez party — 'Opportunity, batch!' — as though this is a normal way to get into clubs
jokes.explanation: 'Opportunity, batch!' is a funny piece of Ilana slang — 'batch' softening 'bitch' as an affectionate address makes it charming. The opportunism is classic Broad City logic: any opening is an opening.

jokes.text: The overheard phone call — Abbi overhearing someone's conversation and immediately deciding they can crash the 40/40 Club VIP party.
jokes.setup: A stranger is on the phone confirming party details at the 40/40 Club.
jokes.punchline: Abbi immediately treats this as a personal invitation and memorizes the details ('Hernandez party, free bottles') to crash it.
jokes.explanation: A good example of the show's resourcefulness comedy — these characters turn any situation into an opportunity. Not a craft joke but good situational comedy.

jokes.text: Good evening, good sir. We are with the Hernandez party.
jokes.setup: They approach the bouncer to crash the club event
jokes.punchline: Ilana's 'Good evening, good sir' formality — affecting aristocratic manners to bluff their way into a party they have no connection to
jokes.explanation: The tonal mismatch of 'good sir' at a hip-hop club is a consistent Ilana move — performing white-bread formality in contexts that don't call for it. Functional.

jokes.text: God, we are making money while we're clubbing.
jokes.setup: They're dancing at the club, having crashed successfully
jokes.punchline: The reminder that their partying is technically a financial strategy — they're 'making money' by not paying rent so their subletters can pay them
jokes.explanation: A nice structural callback to the episode premise — they've converted clubbing into capitalism. The irony that they're 'making money' by being broke is fun but not deeply constructed.

jokes.text: In da klerb, we all fam. / What? / In da klerb. We all fam. / I don't... / In the club, we are all family. / Are you racist? / N-no. / It's 2016.
jokes.setup: Abbi and Ilana are trying to blend in and Ilana accosts strangers using AAVE she has no claim to
jokes.punchline: When called out, the person asks if she's racist; Ilana stammers 'N-no'; the person drops 'It's 2016' — a perfectly calibrated non-sequitur that acknowledges the weirdness without resolving it
jokes.explanation: 'It's 2016' as the closer is beautifully opaque — what does that mean? What is its argument? The show deliberately doesn't resolve it. The exchange calls out Ilana's cultural-appropriation habits without being preachy, using the stranger's reaction as the editorial. The stammered 'N-no' is a great performance beat.

jokes.text: Mami Lani wanna climb da beanstalk.
jokes.setup: Ilana spots someone tall/attractive at the club
jokes.punchline: Refers to herself in the third person as 'Mami Lani' and uses the fairy tale 'beanstalk' as a euphemism for the tall man — delivered with complete conviction
jokes.explanation: 'Mami Lani' as a self-assigned nickname and the beanstalk metaphor are both pure Ilana verbal invention. The third-person construction adds confidence/absurdity. Quotable.

jokes.text: Ab, look... My security. / Gingers have souls! / No, I know they do, dude.
jokes.setup: Ilana has apparently acquired a ginger bodyguard at the club
jokes.punchline: Abbi's defensive 'Gingers have souls!' is a reference to the South Park meme — and Ilana's 'No, I know they do, dude' corrects the misread earnestly, as if it was a reasonable concern
jokes.explanation: The South Park reference earns a smile and the 'No, I know they do, dude' correction is funny but it's a pop culture crutch more than crafted comedy.

jokes.text: That is Blake Griffin! He plays for the N... B... / A! / A? Oh, my God, no way!
jokes.setup: Ilana introduces her tall new friend
jokes.punchline: She starts spelling out NBA and both of them finish it in rising excitement — demonstrating that they know just enough about basketball to be impressed
jokes.explanation: The spelled-out acronym as a shared realization — N... B... A! — with rising intonation is a good performance beat. Using the actual Blake Griffin is a real-celebrity cameo comedy play.

jokes.text: I bet he'd be a stamp in my sexual passport, 'cause you know, I've never been with someone over 6'5".
jokes.setup: Ilana is excited about meeting Blake Griffin
jokes.punchline: 'Sexual passport' — Ilana has a metaphorical passport of sexual experiences organized by category, and height is apparently a category with its own stamp
jokes.explanation: 'Sexual passport' is great Ilana vocabulary — the bureaucratic organization of sexual experience, the implication that there are specific categories requiring documentation, the pride of collecting stamps. Highly quotable and uniquely hers as a character.

jokes.text: What if I make this a threesome? / I mean... One second. / You know, I really pictured it just us first, experimenting, but to have a dick around, I'm down. I don't think it takes away anything from our friendship.
jokes.setup: Ilana proposes a threesome to Blake Griffin
jokes.punchline: Blake Griffin's response is to consider it seriously — 'I mean... One second' — then conclude the dick is fine and it won't damage the friendship, which is an absurd thing for him to say about the friendship between two women he just met
jokes.explanation: Blake Griffin gamely playing a version of himself who thoughtfully considers the interpersonal dynamics of a threesome he's been invited into is very funny. 'I don't think it takes away anything from our friendship' — assuring two strangers about their friendship — is a good absurdist beat.

jokes.text: Hold up. You have a hot piece already in your apartment who's obviously obsessed with you? / We have been texting, like, texts that could be sexts. / Also, he touched my nipple.
jokes.setup: Ilana is redirecting Abbi from the Blake Griffin threesome back to her own sexual opportunity
jokes.punchline: Abbi builds the case incrementally — 'texts that could be sexts' is already hedged enough to be funny, then 'Also, he touched my nipple' lands as the clincher, delivered like a minor supporting detail rather than major evidence
jokes.explanation: The 'also' before 'he touched my nipple' is doing all the work — treating a nipple touch as an afterthought in a chain of evidence. The escalation from hedged texts to definitive physical contact is good structural comedy.

jokes.text: You should go home and satisfy your bon appétit! / You know what? I am gonna go, 'cause I haven't had sex in a while, and also, this place is, like... / Really making me horny. / This plan rules.
jokes.setup: Ilana is encouraging Abbi to go home and sleep with her subletter
jokes.punchline: Abbi's reason for going is split between genuine sexual drought and admitting the club itself is arousing — the club is doing its job
jokes.explanation: 'Bon appétit' as a sexual encouragement is cute Ilana food-sex conflation. Abbi's two-part reasoning is honest and funny in its mundanity. 'This plan rules' is a good button.

jokes.text: Ilana, also, don't forget to stretch! / You got it.
jokes.setup: Abbi's parting advice as Ilana prepares to sleep with Blake Griffin
jokes.punchline: References the earlier dynamic stretching conversation, now clearly a euphemism for sex prep — and Ilana's assured 'You got it' confirms she understands exactly
jokes.explanation: Clean callback construction — the euphemism established earlier gets deployed at the perfect moment. Brief, earned, lands with the audience who caught the setup.

jokes.text: Metro Units, 34th and Steinway. Unidentified black male pretending to read book on park bench, probably armed. Let's roll! / Bye?
jokes.setup: Police radio crackles as Abbi and Ilana deal with the robbery aftermath
jokes.punchline: The dispatch describes 'unidentified black male pretending to read book' as a probable threat — the absurdity of 'pretending to read' as suspicious behavior; Ilana's confused 'Bye?' as the officers rush off
jokes.explanation: The joke is pointed social commentary — police ignoring an actual robbery victim to respond to a Black man reading a book — delivered through Broad City's characteristic matter-of-fact lens. Ilana's 'Bye?' captures the absurdity without editorializing. Effective and sharp.

jokes.text: As an athlete, I just think it's really important to do, like, some dynamic stretching before any kind of physical activity.
jokes.setup: Blake Griffin, NBA star, is about to have sex with Ilana
jokes.punchline: He frames it in completely earnest athlete-speak — 'dynamic stretching before physical activity' — as though this is a routine athletic protocol
jokes.explanation: The sports-interview vernacular ('dynamic stretching,' 'physical activity') applied to sex is well-executed — the joke is that he's not using euphemisms at all, he's entirely sincere about stretching, and yet the context makes it a double entendre. Rewatch bonus for leading to the Abbi callback.

jokes.text: What if we dynamically... um... take our clothes off? / Yes, I'm down. / Awesome. / I'm so down. / Okay, go.
jokes.setup: Blake Griffin's stretching suggestion gives Ilana an opening
jokes.punchline: Her rephrasing of undressing as 'dynamically taking clothes off' — adopting his athletic language for a strip — and their mutual rapid affirmations, ending in 'Okay, go'
jokes.explanation: Clean extension of the stretching joke — Ilana adopts his vocabulary back at him. The mutual 'I'm down / Awesome / I'm so down / Okay, go' has a great rhythm — the rapid reciprocal confirmations that build to 'Okay, go' as a sports command.

jokes.text: Oh, my God! That is unreal. You are joking. Is... something wrong? / Oh, my God, no! The opposite of it. / It's so much. So much good. / I mean... / But it's not gonna, um, work? / I won't be able to... receive you.
jokes.setup: Ilana and Blake Griffin begin undressing
jokes.punchline: Ilana's reaction to Blake Griffin's anatomy — the escalating superlatives ('That is unreal. You are joking.') followed by her regretful conclusion that it's too much to 'receive' — is pure escalation comedy with the punchline withheld but completely legible
jokes.explanation: 'So much good' is a great construction — the incomplete grammar conveying overload. The reversal from enthusiasm to 'I won't be able to receive you' is a perfect anti-climax that still makes the point emphatically. Strong structure: setup-escalate-deflate-button.

jokes.text: But what if we get... creative? / Like, what do you have in mind? / I don't know. But we are gonna find out.
jokes.setup: The sexual impasse seems to be a dead end
jokes.punchline: Blake Griffin's complete openness to improvisation — 'I don't know, but we are gonna find out' — delivered with athlete-level confidence about sexual problem-solving
jokes.explanation: The solution being entirely undetermined but enthusiastically committed to is a good button. Blake Griffin's 'we are gonna find out' has game-time energy applied to sex that's consistently funny throughout this subplot.

jokes.text: Sorry, Ab, the next bus out of Atlantic City isn't 'til morning. I could FaceTime with you and watch you sleep if that'll help. / All right, bye. / What... What are we doing? What's this relationship?
jokes.setup: Abbi asks Bevers to come back early for moral support after the robbery
jokes.punchline: He can't make it, but offers to FaceTime and watch her sleep — replicating exactly the creepy stepdad behavior from earlier in the episode, completely unaware of the echo
jokes.explanation: This is exceptional structural comedy. Bevers offering to FaceTime and watch her sleep is a direct callback to the stepdad-watching-children-sleep story, but Bevers is so oblivious he has no idea. Abbi's 'What... What are we doing? What's this relationship?' is the right response — recognizing the creepiness without naming the parallel. The show trusts the audience to make the connection. Rewatch bonus is high because on second viewing you see the full circle. Strong craft across all components.

jokes.text: Clear! / Seriously, you're all clear. / Thanks, Trey. / No problem. I've always wanted to do that. You know, 'clear'? / Right.
jokes.setup: Trey has dramatically swept Abbi's apartment after the robbery, calling 'clear' in each room
jokes.punchline: After the intense security sweep, he immediately reveals his motivation was entirely personal — he's always wanted to say 'clear' — reducing his heroism to a childhood fantasy fulfilled
jokes.explanation: The sincerity of 'I've always wanted to do that. You know, clear?' is very Trey — a fundamentally sweet, slightly dim guy who showed up to help his crush and got a bucket-list moment out of it. The deadpan 'Right' from Abbi is perfect.

jokes.text: You don't give your key to a stranger just 'cause he's really cute, you know? / That's a dumb idea. / Hey, hey, hey, hey. It's cool.
jokes.setup: Abbi is processing the robbery and begins to self-blame
jokes.punchline: Her self-criticism is entirely about the attractiveness logic — not 'don't give keys to strangers' but 'don't give keys to strangers BECAUSE THEY'RE CUTE' — and then she immediately says 'that's a dumb idea' validating her own criticism while Trey tries to console her
jokes.explanation: Functional character beat — Abbi identifying the exact wrong part of her mistake as the lesson learned. Trey's consolation is sweet but unremarkable.

jokes.text: 'Hangover III,' honestly, the best one. / 'Babe.' Oscar nom. / And my personal favorite, 'Ratatouille.'
jokes.setup: Trey offers to pop in a movie from his personal collection to cheer Abbi up
jokes.punchline: His three-movie lineup reveals his taste: The Hangover III (which was critically panned), Babe (a 1995 pig movie), and Ratatouille as his 'personal favorite' — an oddly perfect and specific set of films that characterize Trey completely
jokes.explanation: The specificity of this DVD collection is excellent character writing. 'Hangover III, honestly the best one' is wrong in the way a well-meaning gym bro would be wrong. 'Babe. Oscar nom.' is perfect — the abbreviated review, the prestige justification, the correct but contextually absurd citation. Ratatouille as the personal favorite is the kicker — it's genuinely a great film, which makes Trey more likable while still being absurd company for the others.

jokes.text: Oh, that'd be cool, but, like, he took my TV and my DVD player. / I anticipated that, so... / My mom got me this portable DVD player for Christmas so I could watch movies on the subway.
jokes.setup: Abbi points out she has no TV because the subletter stole it
jokes.punchline: Trey anticipated this contingency — not because he's clever but because he owns a portable DVD player his mom gave him for the subway, which he brought as a genuine solution
jokes.explanation: The detail that his mom bought it for watching movies on the subway is a perfect piece of Trey characterization — sweet, earnest, slightly ridiculous. The visual of two people watching Ratatouille on a tiny portable DVD player in a robbed apartment is implied and excellent.

jokes.text: Man, I wish there was a basketball league for women so I could play. / Well, there is. / It's the... WNBA. / Yeah. / The WNBA. / I mean, they are so good. / I literally steal moves from them all the time.
jokes.setup: Trey expresses a wish about women's basketball
jokes.punchline: The WNBA exists. Has existed for decades. Trey is unaware of it. But then his response to learning about it is immediate, complete, and sincere enthusiasm — he steals moves from them
jokes.explanation: The structure is great: the absurd premise (not knowing WNBA exists) is immediately redeemed by his genuine, effusive admiration for women's basketball. He went from WNBA-ignorant to WNBA-superfan in about two seconds. 'I literally steal moves from them all the time' is the payoff that makes Trey lovable rather than just clueless. 'That's women for you. Always making stuff better' is a great unasked-for feminist button.

jokes.text: If Remy had a real restaurant, I would totally go there for my cheat meals. Don't care that he's a rat. Don't give a shit. He is so... special.
jokes.setup: They're watching Ratatouille, Trey's personal favorite
jokes.punchline: Trey's review of Remy as a chef is heartfelt and goes further than expected — the explicit 'don't give a shit' that he's a rat, then the reverent pause before 'special' — reveals a depth of feeling about a cartoon rat
jokes.explanation: Trey's earnest defense of eating at a rat's restaurant — 'Don't care that he's a rat. Don't give a shit.' — is funny specifically because his concern is about hygiene considerations, not fictional-ness. And then 'He is so... special' with the pause is unexpectedly sweet. Great Trey characterization.

jokes.text: I would say my favorite painter is probably, um... Pablo Picasso. / Picasso, baby. / Uh, yeah, he's, like, pretty classic. / Want to see my impression of a Picasso painting? / Yes, I would love to. / [contorts body into Cubist shape] / Trey, that is really funny. / It kinda hurts.
jokes.setup: They're talking about art and Trey claims Picasso as his favorite painter
jokes.punchline: He immediately offers a physical impression of a Picasso painting — a full-body cubist contortion — and his review of his own performance is 'it kinda hurts'
jokes.explanation: 'It kinda hurts' is the perfect button — the physical comedy is implied (the contortion), but the punchline is his entirely matter-of-fact reporting of the physical toll as a minor drawback. The escalation from 'favorite painter' to 'let me physically embody his style' to 'it hurts' is very well structured. Character-specific to Trey's whole deal.

jokes.text: I was gonna show you a video on my laptop. / And then that guy stole it today. / Stole that, too... / Hey, you know what they say? You're not a real New Yorker until you've been robbed. / I've been a New Yorker three times, so.
jokes.setup: Abbi realizes another stolen item and is about to lament it
jokes.punchline: Trey's silver lining ('not a real New Yorker until you've been robbed') followed by his own tally — 'three times' — delivered with quiet pride as if this is a credential
jokes.explanation: 'I've been a New Yorker three times, so' is an excellent button — interpreting robbery tally as a durability metric, with the slight pride of a survivor. The comma-pause before 'so' does a lot of work. Quotable.

jokes.text: Bazinga.
jokes.setup: Ilana arrives at Abbi's, apparently having appeared unexpectedly
jokes.punchline: 'Bazinga' — the Big Bang Theory catchphrase deployed in a context where it signals 'surprise' — is funny specifically because Ilana would use this phrase unironically
jokes.explanation: The joke is the incongruity of Ilana, a very cool person, using a Big Bang Theory catchphrase. Works as a small character moment.

jokes.text: I am so sorry that my phone was on 'do not disturb.' I love that function.
jokes.setup: Ilana apologizes for missing the robbery emergency
jokes.punchline: Immediately pivots to enthusiastic praise for the do-not-disturb feature that caused her to miss it
jokes.explanation: The apology and the enthusiasm for what caused the problem coexist completely naturally in Ilana's delivery. 'I love that function' — endorsing the technology over the human connection — is a great Ilana priority. Clean and economical.

jokes.text: My mom always said that French people hate Jews. I'm pretty sure it's unrelated, though, right? / Right, he probably didn't know that you were... / Perfect nose.
jokes.setup: They're processing whether Henri targeted Ilana specifically
jokes.punchline: Ilana wonders if antisemitism was a motive; Abbi reassures her 'he probably didn't know you were...' — and Ilana cuts her off with 'Perfect nose' — confirming her Jewish identity through the compliment/insecurity
jokes.explanation: 'Perfect nose' as the self-defining trait — delivered as if it answers 'didn't know you were Jewish' — is funny and specific. The implication that Ilana's nose could identify her Jewishness is the dark subtext, handled with just enough lightness to land as comedy rather than discomfort.

jokes.text: Dude, ew! / Ohhhh! Ew! / What the fuck? / What the fuck? / A fucking condom! / Ab, this is it! This could be the key to the whole crime! This could unlock the whole thing, it's only six to... / Six-and-a-half hours old. / Fresh on his tail.
jokes.setup: They discover a used condom left by Henri
jokes.punchline: Ilana's immediate pivot to crime-solving — 'this is the key to the whole crime!' — treating a used condom as a DNA evidence breakthrough; then the precise age calculation ('six-and-a-half hours old') and 'fresh on his tail'
jokes.explanation: The shared 'ew' escalating to Ilana's CSI mode is a fast, clean joke. 'Six-and-a-half hours old' is funny because it's so precise — she's done the math. 'Fresh on his tail' as a detective-show idiom applied to a sex criminal who robbed an apartment is a good absurdist image.

jokes.text: You didn't see him. He was dangerous. / Really? / Yeah, we gotta just, like, throw that condom away and act like we never saw it and never bring it up again. / You sure? / I am positive, dude! / You didn't see this guy! He had pecs... Like, they were bigger than my boobs!
jokes.setup: Abbi insists they drop the DNA evidence plan
jokes.punchline: The real reason for not pursuing Henri isn't safety — it's that he was too attractive, as evidenced by his pecs being bigger than Abbi's boobs
jokes.explanation: The misdirection of 'dangerous' — legitimate safety concern — deflated by the real reason being 'he was hot.' Abbi's pause before committing to 'He had pecs...' is a good performance beat. The comparison to her own boobs as the benchmark of impressiveness is a nice specific detail.

jokes.text: To be that horny and move so efficiently is something that, you know, even though I hate him, I admire.
jokes.setup: Abbi is processing her complicated feelings about Henri, who robbed her
jokes.punchline: Her conclusion is a grudging professional admiration for the operational efficiency of someone who simultaneously had sex with her subletter while robbing her apartment
jokes.explanation: This is one of the episode's best jokes. The construction is impeccable — the concession ('even though I hate him') before the praise ('I admire') is perfectly hedged, and what she's admiring is the multitasking of crime and sex. The corporate-efficiency framing ('move so efficiently') applied to a thieving seducer is an original angle that could only be Abbi. Highly quotable. Comparable to the 8.0 anchor examples.

jokes.text: So, sex! Sex with Blake Griffin, dude, you didn't even talk about it. / It wasn't full peena-vageena. / Penis in vagina. / That sucks. Is it... Was it okay? / It was awesome. We did stuff that I never even thought of before. All I gotta say, it was nothing but net. / Oh! / And by nothing but net, I mean he fingered me 'til I came.
jokes.setup: Ilana reports back on her night with Blake Griffin using sports terminology
jokes.punchline: 'Nothing but net' as a basketball euphemism for sexual success — followed immediately by the explicit clarification 'and by nothing but net, I mean he fingered me 'til I came' — destroying any ambiguity
jokes.explanation: The 'nothing but net' setup works as a satisfying sports metaphor payoff to the Blake Griffin storyline — it sounds perfect. Then the self-translation 'and by nothing but net I mean he fingered me til I came' is the real punchline: the deliberate over-explanation of the metaphor, spoken with complete casualness. Also a callback to joke 21 (the fingering theme from 'Fear'), which the show has been quietly threading all episode. 'Peena-vageena' is a funny pronunciation choice. High craft and impact.

jokes.text: Lincoln loved it, even. He thought it was so hot. / You told Lincoln? / Was he jealous? / I think he was okay with it.
jokes.setup: Ilana casually mentions Lincoln's response to her Blake Griffin encounter
jokes.punchline: She told Lincoln — the man she's clearly in love with — about sleeping with someone else, and frames his response as 'okay with it,' which is almost certainly understated
jokes.explanation: The Ilana/Lincoln dynamic gets a nice texture moment — her inability to see how much she cares about him even as she seeks his validation about sleeping with someone else. 'I think he was okay with it' is the perfect uncertain hedge.

jokes.text: This is Blake Griffin's shoe. Now I use it as a murse. A male purse. We're Eskimo brothers.
jokes.setup: Ilana produces a large shoe
jokes.punchline: She has Blake Griffin's actual shoe, has repurposed it as a bag, coined 'murse' (male purse) as if this is standard terminology, and declares them 'Eskimo brothers' via the shoe's former owner
jokes.explanation: The logic chain here is very funny: obtained giant shoe → repurposed as bag → coined 'murse' → concluded she and Abbi are 'Eskimo brothers' (the term for people who've slept with the same person). 'We're Eskimo brothers' about two women connected by a celebrity's shoe/sexual encounter is a perfectly absurd conclusion. The visual of the giant shoe as a purse is also a strong visual gag. High quotability.

jokes.text: I'm so happy to be home. I have frosting in every one of my crevices.
jokes.setup: Ilana returns to her apartment after the night's adventures
jokes.punchline: 'Frosting in every one of my crevices' — what happened? Why frosting? The lack of explanation is funnier than any explanation could be
jokes.explanation: The episode ends on this line and it's exactly right — the unexplained state ('frosting in every crevice') implies a wild night while withholding all details. 'Crevices' is a funnier word than 'cracks' or 'places.' Ilana's contentment is complete. High economy and character integration — this is a great closing joke.

jokes.text: What's up, bitch? [Ilana pops out from inside the apartment she was supposedly subletting]
jokes.setup: Throughout the episode, Ilana's apartment has been sublet — she's been displaced all night
jokes.punchline: She emerges from inside her own apartment, having apparently been there all along — or she's gotten back in — which retroactively undermines the entire premise and her insistence on staying out all night
jokes.explanation: The visual button of Ilana in her own apartment that she was supposedly subletting all night is a perfect episode closer structural joke. 'What's up, bitch?' as the only line needed — no explanation given. The rewatch bonus is high because it recontextualizes the whole episode's premise. Pure economy.
